topic: Someone discovers their friend has been lying about their job
raw_output:
[story 1]

The lukewarm coffee did little to sharpen the edges of my afternoon. I watched Liam across the table, his smile bright and practiced as he recounted his latest triumph at the 'consulting firm.' Every word flowed smoothly, a well-rehearsed performance. I nodded along, the movement slow, almost weighted. A small knot tightened in my chest, a familiar, dull pressure. It wasn't anger, exactly; it was more like the slow, heavy sinking of a stone in deep water. I just wanted the tension to stop, to dissipate into the quiet hum of the room. When he finished, I offered a small, agreeable sound. "That sounds… busy," I murmured, my voice lacking any real inflection. I picked at a loose thread on my sweater, focusing intently on the minuscule imperfection, anything to avoid looking directly at the carefully constructed facade he wore.

[story 2]

I found the discrepancy tucked away in an old email chain—a sudden, jarring misalignment in the timeline Liam had presented. The realization didn't hit me with a sudden jolt, but rather like a slow, creeping chill that settled deep in my bones. I reread the correspondence three times, the screen light reflecting dully in my eyes. My hands, resting on the keyboard, felt strangely heavy, unmotivated to type anything further. I looked up at Liam, who was leaning back, radiating an easy confidence that now seemed brittle. I managed a small, neutral expression. "Oh. That's interesting," I said, my tone even, almost flat. I didn't press. The energy to confront him, to shake the foundation of his story, simply wasn't there. It felt exhausting, like trying to lift a boulder with one hand. I just let the information sit there, heavy and unaddressed.

[story 3]

The air in the park felt thick and unmoving, mirroring the state of my own spirit. I was supposed to be excited for Liam’s career advancement, but as he spoke, I felt only a pervasive sense of inertia. My gaze drifted to the distant trees, tracing their shapes with a vacant stare. When the details of his employment began to unravel under my scrutiny, a strange stillness took over. My breathing became shallow, almost imperceptible. I didn't want to disrupt the easy rhythm of our conversation. Instead, I focused on the pattern of the paving stones beneath my feet, counting them mentally, a pointless exercise in meticulousness. "Right," I finally managed, a soft exhale escaping my lips. "I see." The effort it took to maintain that level of placid acceptance felt monumental, draining the last reserves of my will.
